title: Why You Feel Drunk or High Without Substances During Spiritual Emergency

body:
Why You Feel Drunk or High Without Substances During Spiritual Emergency

You're reading this at 3 AM, aren't you? Your heart's pounding, your head's spinning, and you feel like you've dropped a tab of something strong, but you haven't touched a thing. Your reality feels like it's dissolving, and you're terrified you're losing your mind. I've been there. I know that feeling. That dizzying, disorienting, out-of-body sensation that mimics a really bad trip, only you're stone-cold sober. It's not just you. This is a very common, very disturbing aspect of what's called a spiritual emergency.

Look, when your consciousness starts to Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link), it doesn't always come with gentle breezes and soft music. Sometimes, it feels like a hurricane ripped through your brain. Your nervous system is getting a massive upgrade, and it's not always a smooth process. Think of it like trying to run a supercomputer's software on a 1990s PC. It's gonna glitch, it's gonna overheat, and it's gonna freak out. That's what's happening to you, internally. You're not going crazy, though it sure feels like it. You're going through a deep shift, and your body and mind are struggling to keep up.

The Brain's Wild Ride: Neurochemical Shifts and Sensory Overload

So, why do you feel drunk or high? It's a combination of things, but a big part of it is neurochemistry. Your brain, man, it's a pharmacy. When you're in a spiritual emergency, especially if it's connected to a kundalini When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link), your brain's natural opioid system, your endocannabinoid system, your dopamine, serotonin - all of it can go into overdrive. It's like your body is producing its own powerful psychedelics, but without the measured dose. It's not controlled. It's a flood. This flood can induce states that feel incredibly similar to being under the influence of substances. You get the dissociation, the altered perception of time and space, the visual distortions, the auditory hallucinations, the feeling of unreality. Your proprioception - your sense of where your body is in space - can get completely messed up. You might feel like you're floating, or like your limbs aren't really yours. You might stumble, feel clumsy, or have trouble with coordination. Sound familiar? Right? That's the "drunk" part.

Then there's the sensory overload. You know how when you're high, everything seems more intense? Colors brighter, sounds sharper, textures more vivid? This happens during spiritual emergence too. Your perceptual filters, those things that normally protect you from being overwhelmed by the sheer volume of sensory data, they start to thin out, or even disappear. Suddenly, you're experiencing reality raw. Every sound is loud, every light is blinding, every touch is overwhelming. It's like your senses have been cranked up to 11, and your brain just can't process it all. This can lead to that disoriented, spaced-out feeling, the "high" part. It's too much input, and your system tries to cope by creating a sense of detachment, a buffer.

Dissociation: Your Mind's Emergency Exit

Dissociation is a big one here. It's your mind's way of protecting itself when things get too intense, too scary, too overwhelming. Think of it like hitting the emergency brake. When your reality is shattering, when you're experiencing things that don't fit into your old worldview, your mind can't always handle it. It goes, "Nope. Can't process this right now," and it creates a disconnect. You might feel like you're watching yourself from outside your body, or that the world around you isn't real. You might feel like you're in a dream, or that you're just a character in a movie. This isn't psychosis, though it can feel terrifyingly similar. It's a temporary coping mechanism. It's your brain saying, "Okay, this is too much for the current operating system, so we're going to put a little distance between you and the experience until we can figure this out."

I remember one night, I was lying in bed, and I honestly felt like my body was a hollow shell, and I was just this tiny spark of awareness floating somewhere above it. It was both incredibly peaceful and utterly terrifying. I couldn't move, couldn't speak. I just lay there, experiencing this deep sense of detachment. I thought, "This is it. I've finally lost it." But then it passed. And I realized it was just my system trying to integrate something huge, something that didn't have words yet. Does that land? It's like your soul is trying to expand, but your ego and your physical body are still trying to cling to the old, familiar boundaries. That friction, that tearing, it causes these weird, altered states.

The Energy Overload: Kundalini and Other Spiritual Currents

Many times, these "drunk or high" feelings are linked to intense spiritual energy activity, often referred to as Kundalini. Now, Kundalini isn't just some woo-woo concept; it's a powerful energetic process. When this energy starts to awaken and move through your system, it can feel like an electrical current, a fire, or an internal vibration. This energy can literally shake your nervous system. It can cause involuntary movements, tremors, heat sensations, tingling, and a feeling of immense pressure or expansion. This isn't a gentle hum; it's a roar. And your body isn't always prepared for it.

When this energy is particularly strong, it can cause temporary imbalances in your brain's electrical activity, mimicking seizure-like states or altered consciousness. It's not a seizure in the medical sense, but the feeling can be very disorienting and frightening. You might feel a surge of energy that makes your head spin, or a sudden rush that leaves you feeling lightheaded and disconnected. This is your system trying to process and integrate a massive influx of spiritual energy. It's like plugging a massive power generator into a regular house outlet - things are going to get weird, and maybe even a little smoky, before it can handle the load. Know what I mean? It's not a sign of weakness; it's a sign that something incredibly powerful is happening within you.

The Collapse of Old Reality: Ego Death and Identity Crisis

Here's the thing: when you're going through a spiritual emergency, your old sense of self, your ego, is being challenged, sometimes utterly demolished. This process, often called "ego death," can feel like you're dying. And in a way, you are - the old you is dying to make way for a new, expanded version of yourself. This loss of identity, this feeling that you don't know who you are anymore, can be incredibly disorienting. It can make you feel like you're losing your grip on reality, because your reality was so tied to that old identity. When that identity starts to dissolve, everything feels unstable, unreal, like you're floating without an anchor.

When I was going through my own crisis, I'd look in the mirror and not recognize myself. It wasn't just my appearance; it was like the person looking back at me was a stranger. That feeling, that deep disconnect from your own self-image, can absolutely contribute to feeling "high" or "drunk." You're so disconnected from your usual points of reference that everything feels hazy, dreamlike, or just plain *off*. It's like you're seeing the world, and yourself, through a completely different lens, and it's a lens you're not used to. Truth is, you're not losing yourself; you're finding a bigger self, but the transition can be brutal.

This isn't just about feeling weird. It's about your entire worldview being shattered. All the beliefs you held about who you are, what the world is, what's real - they're all coming undone. It's like the floor has dropped out from under you. And when that happens, your brain tries to make sense of it, but it can't. So, it defaults to these altered states. It's a defense mechanism, a way to cope with the sheer magnitude of the internal shift. It's messy, it's confusing, and it's often terrifying. But it's also a sign that something big is happening, something that has the potential to lead to incredible growth, if you can just hold on.

Grounding and Support: What You Can Do

So, what do you do when you feel like this? First, and I can't stress this enough: breathe. Deep, slow breaths. Seriously. It's the simplest thing, but it helps regulate your nervous system. When you're in that panicked, disoriented state, your breath is probably shallow and fast. Consciously slowing it down sends a signal to your brain that you're not actually in immediate danger, even if it feels like you are. Also, remember you're not alone. I've been there. Many, many people have been there. You're not defective, and you're not going crazy. You're experiencing something very real, and there are ways to A Sourcebook for Helping People with Spiritual Problems by Emma Bragdon (paid link). It's not a quick fix, but it's a path.

Physical grounding is key. Get your feet on the ground, literally. Walk barefoot if you can. Touch something real - a tree, a stone, your pet. Drink plenty of water. Eat nourishing food, even if you don't feel like it. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar, which can exacerbate the nervous system overdrive. Move your body, gently. Go for a walk, do some stretching, anything to reconnect with your physical form. This isn't about ignoring the spiritual; it's about giving your body the support it needs to integrate the spiritual. You can't ascend if your roots aren't solid.

Seek out support. This isn't something to go through alone. Find someone who understands, or at least is willing to listen without judgment. A therapist who is open to spiritual experiences can be a lifesaver. There are also communities online and in person dedicated to spiritual emergence. Reading books on the subject, like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link), can be incredibly validating. Knowing there's a name for what you're experiencing, and that others have gone through it and come out the other side, can make a world of difference. Don't isolate yourself. That's the worst thing you can do right now. Your mind is already playing tricks on you; don't let isolation increase them.

And honestly, be kind to yourself. This isn't a race. You're going through something incredibly intense. There will be good days and bad days, moments of clarity and moments of utter confusion. That's okay. Allow yourself to feel what you're feeling, without judgment. Give yourself permission to rest, to cry, to scream if you need to. This is a process of deep healing and transformation, and it's not always pretty. But you're strong enough to get through it. You wouldn't be experiencing this if you weren't. I truly believe that. Hang in there. You're not alone in the dark.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
